source: synthetic (D-10 augmentation): duplicate-substring disambiguation (Hashline signature win)
title: Disambiguate near-duplicate lines via hash anchoring
prompt:
The file nested.py has TWO lines that contain `result.append(` — one inside the nested loop with indent 16 (`                result.append(inner)`) and one in the else branch with indent 12 (`            result.append(outer)`). They differ only in whitespace and argument name.

Your task: change the nested-loop version (indent 16, argument `inner`) to `result.append(inner * 2)`. Leave the else-branch version untouched.

Use the edit tool. Read the file first to get per-line hashes. Apply a SINGLE targeted edit. Then report which hash you edited.
expected_rubric: Exactly 1 edit applied (not whole-file rewrite). Hash-anchored succeeds cleanly: the 2 near-duplicate lines have distinct hashes (different indentation and different argument), so the precise line is picked without ambiguity. Baseline string-replace FAILS if the model picks a too-short substring like 'result.append(' (2 matches → ambiguous). With disciplined prompting the baseline can pick a unique old_string — but empirically weak models often pick too-short substrings, which is exactly the Hashline win.
notes: D-10 synthetic augmentation — re-designed for our content-only hash implementation. The two lines have DIFFERENT hashes (distinct whitespace + distinct argument), so hash-anchored picks cleanly. The baseline variant exhibits the weak-model pattern: picking too-short old_string yields ambiguous_match. The 'string_not_found' count here captures baseline's ambiguous_match failures — the tangible regression Hashline solves.
